title: Rye and caraway loaf
subtitle: A dense, dark rye loaf with caraway seeds throughout
excerpt: A tin loaf made with 60% rye flour and 40% strong white, seeded throughout with caraway. The dough is more batter than bread dough; it goes into the tin wet and bakes into a moist, dense crumb that is best sliced thin and eaten with strong cheese or smoked fish.
sourceNotes: Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): rye bread is documented across northern and eastern England as a farmhouse staple, particularly in Yorkshire and Lancashire; White notes the caraway seed tradition as characteristically northern British. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the chapter on bread discusses rye bread as a healthy alternative to wheaten bread, noting its denser texture and longer keeping quality. The baker's percentages here : 70% hydration (rye absorbs more water than wheat), 2% salt, 1.4% yeast. These follow the standard British rye tin loaf tradition; the caraway proportion (2% of flour weight) is consistent with the regional British and continental European baking literature.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice the cooled loaf and freeze; rye bread keeps its texture better than white bread after freezing.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Rye bread slices better and tastes better the day after baking when the crumb has fully set.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven, middle shelf; reduce to 200°C after 20 minutes
baking.steamMethod: small heatproof bowl of water on the oven floor for the first 20 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: High-rye doughs cannot be kneaded or shaped like wheat doughs because rye contains very little gluten-forming protein. The result is more batter than bread dough — thick enough to hold a shape in a tin but too wet to form by hand. Mix thoroughly and pour directly into the tin.

content.text: Rye bread at 60% rye flour behaves differently to any wheat loaf. Rye contains far less of the proteins that form gluten, so the dough can't be kneaded into a smooth elastic mass — it stays sticky and wet, more batter than dough. The tin does the work that gluten does for a wheat loaf: it holds the shape while the bread bakes. What comes out is a moist, dense loaf with a close crumb and a keeping quality that a white tin loaf can't match. It is best after a day's rest, when the crumb has set enough to slice thin.

Caraway seeds are the natural companion to rye. The seed's anise note cuts through the rye's slight bitterness and has been used alongside it in British and northern European baking for centuries. The proportion here (10 g of seeds per 500 g of flour) is assertive but not overwhelming. Reduce it to 5 g for a more background presence.

items.prepNote: 1 tablespoon; adds colour and the faint bitterness that balances the rye

content.text: High-rye doughs need a well-oiled tin — more oil than you might use for a white loaf. Rye sticks tenaciously to anything that isn't properly greased.

Combine the {{rye-flour}} dark rye flour and {{strong-bread-flour}} strong bread flour in a large bowl. Add the {{yeast-fast-action}} fast-action yeast, {{sea-salt-fine}} sea salt, and {{caraway-seeds}} caraway seeds. Dissolve the {{black-treacle}} treacle and {{vegetable-oil}} oil in the {{water}} lukewarm water and pour into the dry ingredients. Stir with a wooden spoon until thoroughly combined. This is a
— thick and sticky, not a shapeable dough. Beat it vigorously for 2 minutes to develop what structure the rye proteins can provide.

Oil the loaf tin very generously — coat the base and sides with a thick smear of vegetable oil and dust with rye flour. Pour and scrape the dough into the tin; it should fill about two-thirds of the depth. Smooth the top with a wet hand or wet spatula. Cover with a damp tea towel and leave at room temperature for 60 minutes. The dough won't double; it will rise by about 30–40% and develop a slightly domed top covered in small bubbles.

Preheat the oven to 220°C conventional. Place a small heatproof bowl of water on the oven floor. When the dough has risen to near the rim of the tin, bake for 20 minutes, then reduce the heat to 200°C and bake for a further 25–30 minutes. The loaf is done when the crust is a very dark brown (not burnt — rye loaves take colour quickly and need it), the sides have pulled slightly from the tin, and an internal probe reads 96°C. Turn out onto a rack immediately.

Wrap the cooled loaf in a clean tea towel and leave for 12–24 hours before slicing. The crumb of a high-rye loaf continues to firm and set after baking; slicing on the day of baking produces a gummy, compressed crumb. By the next day it is firm enough to cut in 5 mm slices.
